file_name: Araújo et al. - 2021 - Automatic segmentation of melanoma skin cancer using transfer learning and fine-tuning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (U-net and LinkNet) combined with transfer learning and fine-tuning techniques to perform automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions in dermoscopic images from the PH2, ISIC 2018, and DermIS datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (U-net and LinkNet) with transfer learning and fine-tuning for melanoma segmentation. It reports specific quantitative results (Dice scores) from experiments conducted on three datasets (PH2, ISIC 2018, and DermIS) and includes a methodology, results, and conclusion section typical of primary research.

title: Automatic segmentation of melanoma skin cancer using transfer learning and fine‑tuning
brief_summary: This study developed an automatic segmentation method for melanoma skin lesions in dermoscopic images using U-net and LinkNet deep learning networks combined with transfer learning and fine-tuning techniques. The researchers evaluated the models' ability to generalize by testing them on three public datasets: PH2, ISIC 2018, and DermIS, including cross-dataset experiments to assess whether the models learned disease characteristics or dataset-specific features. The U-net architecture demonstrated superior performance compared to LinkNet across all configurations. Key results showed that U-net achieved average Dice scores of 0.923 on the PH2 dataset, 0.893 on ISIC 2018, and 0.879 on the DermIS dataset. The study concluded that while fine-tuning significantly improved accuracy, training on a single small dataset like PH2 limited generalization, whereas the ISIC 2018 dataset allowed the model to better adapt to the disease characteristics.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for segmentation. The PH2 dataset contains 40 melanoma images, ISIC 2018 contains 519 melanoma images, and DermIS contains 119 melanoma images.

Used as the negative class in the datasets. The PH2 dataset contains 160 non-melanoma images, ISIC 2018 contains 2075 non-melanoma images, and DermIS contains 88 non-melanoma images. The study focuses on binary segmentation (lesion vs. background) rather than multi-class classification of specific non-melanoma types.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma skin lesion regions in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions using U-net and LinkNet deep learning networks. The task involves isolating the lesion region from the background in dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as Dice Coefficient (DSC), Jaccard Index (JAC), Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, and AUC, comparing predicted segmentation masks against ground truth masks provided by specialists.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public database for the analysis of dermoscopic images, containing images of melanocytic lesions with segmentation masks made by a specialist doctor.

use.training: Used for training U-net and LinkNet models, either alone or in combination with other datasets.
use.validation: Used for validation/testing in cross-dataset experiments.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance, including cross-dataset generalization tests.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 dataset, an international effort to improve melanoma diagnosis, publicized in a competition for segmentation and classification.

use.training: Used for training U-net and LinkNet models, either alone or in combination with other datasets.
use.validation: Used for validation/testing in cross-dataset experiments.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance, including cross-dataset generalization tests.

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: The Dermatology Information System, a service offering information about dermatology and an atlas of images of skin diseases, from which images were gathered.

use.training: Used for training U-net and LinkNet models, either alone or in combination with other datasets.
use.validation: Used for validation/testing in cross-dataset experiments.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance, including cross-dataset generalization tests.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To increase the reliability of results when the training base is the same as the test base.
To compute segmentation metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, AUC, Dice Coefficient, Jaccard Index) by comparing predicted segmentation masks with specialist-provided ground truth masks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean Dice scores, Jaccard Index, etc.) and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, but no statistical significance testing was performed or reported.

PH2.Notes: U-net with fine-tuning achieved the highest DSC (0.923) on PH2. Training on PH2 alone resulted in poor generalization to other datasets (e.g., DSC=0.591 on DermIS with U-net). Combining datasets for training improved generalization.

ISIC 2018.Notes: Training on ISIC 2018 allowed the model to better adapt to disease characteristics compared to PH2. U-net generally outperformed LinkNet. Fine-tuning provided marginal improvements over transfer learning for ISIC 2018.

DermIS.Notes: U-net with fine-tuning achieved a DSC of 0.879 on DermIS. Training on DermIS alone resulted in poor generalization to PH2 (DSC=0.666 with U-net). Combining DermIS with ISIC 2018 for training improved results on DermIS testing (DSC=0.810 with U-net fine-tuning).

architecture_details.u_net_structure: Contraction path (encoder) composed of pre-trained ResNet34 backbone without the fully connected layer; expansion path (decoder) combines feature maps from contraction stage with symmetric counterparts in expansion stage
architecture_details.linknet_structure: Encoder (left) and decoder (right); encoder formed by pre-trained ResNet34 backbone without fully connected layer; decoder uses full convolution technique; connections between encoders and decoders divert input of each encoding layer to output of corresponding decoder
architecture_details.layers: Number of layers determined by the chosen ResNet34 backbone

hyperparameters.learning_rate: Shallow learning rate used during fine-tuning
hyperparameters.fine_tuning_strategy: Freezing encoder weights and training only the randomly initialized decoder

training_details.training_schedule: Transfer learning stage followed by fine-tuning stage; K-fold cross-validation used for reliability

classification_or_output_head.details: Binary segmentation of lesion region vs background

preprocessing_techniques: The paper did not report specific preprocessing techniques applied to the images (e.g., resizing, normalization, hair removal) prior to modeling. The authors explicitly state in the 'Conclusion and future works' section that they 'have not investigated the impact of pre-processing and data augmentation to reduce computational cost' and identify this as a shortcoming to be addressed in future work.
conclusions: The U-net architecture demonstrated superior segmentation performance compared to LinkNet across all tested configurations and datasets.

Fine-tuning significantly improved segmentation accuracy for both U-net and LinkNet models, with notable increases in Dice scores for cross-dataset generalization tasks (e.g., a 7.4% increase for PH2/DermIS and 7.8% for DermIS/PH2 with U-net).

Training on the small PH2 dataset alone resulted in poor generalization to other datasets (e.g., DSC of 0.591 on DermIS), indicating that the model learned dataset-specific characteristics rather than general disease features.

The ISIC 2018 dataset, due to its larger size and diversity, allowed the model to better adapt to disease characteristics, resulting in better generalization to other datasets compared to training on PH2 or DermIS alone.

Combining multiple datasets (PH2, ISIC 2018, and DermIS) for training improved the consistency and robustness of generalization, although the absolute performance metrics were not always higher than training on the best-performing single dataset (ISIC 2018) for in-distribution testing.
The proposed method achieved competitive results compared to state-of-the-art works, particularly in Dice Coefficient and Jaccard Index metrics on the PH2 and DermIS datasets.

The study did not investigate the impact of pre-processing and data augmentation, identifying this as a limitation and area for future work to reduce computational cost and potentially improve results.
important_remarks: The study did not investigate the impact of pre-processing or data augmentation techniques, explicitly stating this as a limitation to reduce computational cost, which may affect generalization and handling of unbalanced classes.

Training on the small PH2 dataset (40 melanoma images) resulted in poor generalization to other datasets (e.g., DSC=0.591 on DermIS), indicating the model learned dataset-specific artifacts rather than disease characteristics.

The ISIC 2018 dataset demonstrated superior generalization capabilities due to its larger size and diversity, allowing the model to better adapt to disease characteristics compared to PH2 or DermIS alone.

The paper acknowledges potential bias related to skin color diversity, noting that the white population has a significantly higher risk of melanoma, which may limit the reproducibility of results across different populations.

The device used to capture images (resolution, lighting, sharpness) influences image characteristics, and the study did not validate performance on images captured by devices other than those in the used datasets.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results were evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean Dice scores, Jaccard Index) and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods.
The study focused on binary segmentation (lesion vs. background) and did not evaluate the downstream impact of segmentation errors on melanoma classification tasks.
The input images were resized to 128x128 pixels, which the authors note maintains proportions but may distort skin lesion details compared to using original resolution.
